critic_raw:
The SKS hate speech detection paper has several important methodological concerns.

1. The multi-task learning benefit (adding sentiment analysis) is evaluated without controlling for the total number of parameters and training examples. A model trained with sentiment labels has access to more supervision signal than a single-task model. The performance improvement from multi-task training could be entirely due to additional labeled data rather than to any information sharing between sentiment and hate speech detection. The paper should compare against a single-task model trained on equivalent total supervision (e.g., augmented hate speech labels via pseudo-labeling).

2. The category embeddings from a dictionary of derogatory words introduce a lexical lookup that is domain-specific. The paper claims performance improvements from category embeddings, but does not report whether the dictionary is aligned with the vocabulary of the evaluation datasets. If evaluation dataset vocabulary substantially overlaps with the dictionary, the improvement may reflect lexical memorization rather than learned semantic generalization. A test on out-of-vocabulary hate speech would distinguish these.

3. The multi-head attention mechanism in SKS applies attention over both text tokens and sentiment/category signals. The paper does not report which attention heads specialize in which signal type. Without this, it is impossible to verify that the model actually uses sentiment information to improve hate speech detection, as opposed to simply having a larger and more expressive model. Attention head specialization could be verified by ablating individual heads.

4. The benchmark datasets for hate speech detection (e.g., Founta et al., Davidson et al.) have known annotation artifacts: they were collected via keyword search for hate-related terms, creating lexical biases. Models that exploit these biases score well on the benchmark but fail on naturalistically collected hate speech. The paper does not test SKS on a dataset collected without keyword-based sampling.

5. Hate speech detection is a highly imbalanced classification problem (hate speech is typically a small minority of social media posts). The paper's reported accuracy metrics may be dominated by performance on the majority (non-hate) class. F1 score on the minority (hate) class and precision-recall curves are more informative than accuracy in this setting.
all_issues_raised: Multi-task benefit not controlled for additional supervision signal from sentiment labels
Category embedding improvement may reflect lexical memorization of dictionary vocabulary in evaluation data
No attention head specialization analysis to verify sentiment information is actually used for hate detection
Evaluation datasets collected via keyword search, creating lexical biases not tested with naturalistic data
Accuracy metrics dominated by majority class in imbalanced hate speech setting; F1 on hate class not emphasized
Positive transfer assumption between sentiment and hate speech is not validated; negative transfer on non-negative-sentiment hate speech not analyzed
Category embeddings from derogatory word dictionary may constitute indirect label leakage; not ablated on examples lacking derogatory terms
Two-dataset evaluation limits scope; no cross-dataset generalization test across different platforms or communities
Multi-head attention architecture contribution not ablated from multi-task objective contribution
Sentiment labels used for auxiliary training may have systematic errors on ironic or coded hate speech text
Multi-task benefit is not isolated from data augmentation effect; no ablation controls for additional sentiment training data without the multi-task objective
Category embeddings from derogatory word dictionary introduce dataset-specific lexicon dependency that may inflate benchmark performance without generalizing to novel domains
Evaluation on two benchmarks may reflect annotation artifacts rather than genuine hate speech semantics; no cross-dataset or out-of-domain evaluation
Attention-based interpretability is unreported; high attention weights may reflect spurious correlations rather than genuine hate speech semantics
Statistical significance of multi-task improvements is not reported across seeds; class imbalance causes high variance that single-run comparisons cannot capture
